coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of CQD/g-C3N4 as a highly effective metal-free photocatalyst for the degradation of carmoisine and indigo carmine dye
coredata.dc:creator: Sunil, Silpa
Mandal, Badal Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The development of extensive dye pollution in the water ecosystem seriously endangers the health of the living organism. For effectively eradicating dyecontaminants from water bodies, photocatalysis is consideredan effective, energy-consumption, inexpensive disinfection technique. As an alternative to conventional metal-based catalysts, heterogeneous metal-free photocatalysts are more sustainable and kinder to the environment. Here, we reported the simplehydrothermalchemical production of Carbon Quantum dots (CQD)-doped graphitic carbon nitride(GCN). Analytical instruments such as XRD, SEM, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, EDX, FT-IR, thesurface area of BET analysis, photoluminescence, and UV–vis spectroscopy, zeta potentialwere used to characterize the as-prepared CQD doped GCN (CDCN). The degradation studies reveal that the CDCN catalyst displays the highest rate of degradation performance than pure GCN. Within 60 min, it shows 96 % degradation toward indigo Carmine (IC) and 93 % decomposition towards carmoisine dye (CM). Significant e−/h+ separation, an increased surface area, and a high redox potential capacity to induce charge bears may all contribute to the CDCN catalyst’s enhanced photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The efficiency of the photocatalytic process was optimized by studying and altering many variables. These include Dye concentration, catalyst concentration, and variation of the pH solution were some of these. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent stability after three successive runs of the photocatalytic procedure. According to the kinetics analysis results indicate that the photocatalytic decomposition of Indigo Carmine (IC) and carmoisine (CM) dye follows pseudo-first-order kinetics. For better photodegradation performance, a potential photocatalytic method employing several pairs of electron-hole acceptor scavengershas been put forth. Based on the positionsof the band gap and the result of the characterization, a feasible mechanismpathway for charge carriers was also presented.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of CQD/g-C3N4 as a highly effective metal-free photocatalyst for the degradation of carmoisine and indigo carmine dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of extensive dye pollution in the water ecosystem seriously endangers the health of the living organism. For effectively eradicating dyecontaminants from water bodies, photocatalysis is consideredan effective, energy-consumption, inexpensive disinfection technique. As an alternative to conventional metal-based catalysts, heterogeneous metal-free photocatalysts are more sustainable and kinder to the environment. Here, we reported the simplehydrothermalchemical production of Carbon Quantum dots (CQD)-doped graphitic carbon nitride(GCN). Analytical instruments such as XRD, SEM, FE-SEM, HR-TEM, EDX, FT-IR, thesurface area of BET analysis, photoluminescence, and UV–vis spectroscopy, zeta potentialwere used to characterize the as-prepared CQD doped GCN (CDCN). The degradation studies reveal that the CDCN catalyst displays the highest rate of degradation performance than pure GCN. Within 60 min, it shows 96 % degradation toward indigo Carmine (IC) and 93 % decomposition towards carmoisine dye (CM). Significant e−/h+ separation, an increased surface area, and a high redox potential capacity to induce charge bears may all contribute to the CDCN catalyst’s enhanced photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The efficiency of the photocatalytic process was optimized by studying and altering many variables. These include Dye concentration, catalyst concentration, and variation of the pH solution were some of these. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent stability after three successive runs of the photocatalytic procedure. According to the kinetics analysis results indicate that the photocatalytic decomposition of Indigo Carmine (IC) and carmoisine (CM) dye follows pseudo-first-order kinetics. For better photodegradation performance, a potential photocatalytic method employing several pairs of electron-hole acceptor scavengershas been put forth. Based on the positionsof the band gap and the result of the characterization, a feasible mechanismpathway for charge carriers was also presented.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor catalysis is an exciting area in nanotechnology and is mainly focused on creating environmentally friendly, efficient, and long-lasting photocatalysts to handle the worsening of ecological issues and the decline of different types of energy resources. Since the 18th century, there has been a lot of water pollution because dangerous intractable mixtures and contaminated material wastes are being discharged into various reservoirs. In addition to other pollutants, the extreme usage of dyestuffs from several industrial processes leads to severe environmental problems on a global scale. The presence of dye content in water is the main reason for water pollution, and their accumulation in even small amounts can have undesirable impactson living beings. The dye concentration in wastewater from industries is often below 1 ppm [1–3], making their removal even more of a challenge. The processes of physical adsorption, bio-degradation, precipitation of chemicals, and photocatalytic decomposition have all been developed to get rid of dyes despite their chemical inertness [4,5]. The use of photocatalytic degradation as a dye mitigation strategy is seen as a low-cost and long-term option.

Indigo Carmine (IC) is a common colorant utilized by numerous sectors, from food and drink to printing and paper to beauty products and footwear to healthcare goods and garments [6]. It also has some clinical uses, suchas in tests of renal health as well as for detecting tumors [7] Indigo carmine dye is most commonlyutilized in industries for dying fabric. However, approximately thirtypercentof the leftover colour is discharged straight toward the surroundings as wastewater. Since the spilled dye is poisonous, mutagenic, and possibly carcinogenic and causes irreversible eye damage, skin and eye itchiness, vomiting, and diarrhea [8–10], it poses a significant hazard to the environment. Carmoisine is an artificial azo-based dye that imparts a red tint to foodstuffs. Dye is polar and water-soluble because it contains sulfonic groups. Jelly, jams, sweets, and preservatives are all examples of foods that make use of this coloring agent [11]. Many developed nations have outlawed the use of carmoisine because it contains b-naphthylamine, a recognized carcinogen formed when azo groups are reduced. Due to this colour, many people may develop medication sensitivity orallergies. Additionally, aggravating behavioral difficulties in kids include impulsiveness and tiredness, high levels of these dyescan make people unconscious, which may lead to death. Hence, researchers must concentrate on studies regarding Indigo carmine extraction from wastewater adopting a practical approach that produces no byproducts [12–14] . Therefore, many techniques have been developed to eliminate these dyes from effluents from the textile industry, including adsorption, oxidation by chemicals, photoelectron oxidation,and straight ultraviolet (UV) treatment. These methods, however, result in just a transfer of the pollution from a particular stage to the next; they do not lead to its complete degradation. In addition, these techniques have a huge expense and need refinements [15–18].

Graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) is an appealingphotocatalyst [19] because of its excellent catalytic activity as well as its sufficient band gap that is suitable for absorbing UV light (2.7 eV). Excellent thermal energy, high stability, reasonable bandgap value, and cheap manufacturing expenses with the availability of inexpensive C and N components make this organic semiconductor an essential subject of research all over the world. In particular, these tri-s-triazine units can produce conjugated graphitic planes. It is put together via sp2 hybridization. Furthermore, the photogenerated charge has a hard time moving from one ring unit to the next because bridging the atoms of nitrogen with triple coordination does not take part in either the band called valence or conduction. This may lead to low photocatalytic activity because photogenerated electrons as well as holes can be quickly connected in each ring. Additionally, in bulk g-C3N4, the electrical coupling between conjugated planes is limited due to the stacking effect of a low force called van der Waals. Several approacheshave been designed to overcome those constraints, and boost photocatalytic activity; these include noble metal deposition [20], elemental doping [21], dye sensitivity [22], regulated morphology [23], semiconductor coupling [24–26], etc. However, despite significant advancements in the synthesis ofGCN, the photocatalytic efficacy of GCN is severely constrained by the weak van der Waals connections between neighbouring CN layers.

Carbon Quantum dots (CQD)are a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials made of carbon that have similar optical features to quantum dots andappealing characteristics including excellent stability, strong thermalconductivity, minimal toxicity, and compatibility with the environment [27–30]. CQDs are a luminous, potentially zero-dimensional category of carbon materials made of weakly crystalline, amorphous forms of carbon centers (sp2/sp3) having diameters smaller than 10 nm [31] . CQDs feature fascinating luminescence activity, as well as remarkable solubility at the level of a nanoscale [32], strong thermal stability [33], and high compatibility with the environment [34–36]. Together, these characteristics make CQDs affordable, secure,nontoxic, and inexpensivenanodotswith numerousapplications [37]. Similarly, the simplicity of mass manufacture from biological resources improves their application variety [38]. In the studies of catalysis, CQDs have yet to have their catalytic potential fully demonstrated, especially when compared to graphene-based carbonmaterials like carbon nanotubes and others [35,39,40].

We present an earth-abundant, metal-free CQD-dopedGCN with all of these advantages. Compared to GCN, the CQD-dopedGCNcomposite showed significantly higher photocatalytic activity towards IC and CM dye degradation caused by UV, Visible, and sunlight exposure. The CQD/GCN(CDCN) materials’ metal-free and environmentally benign features make it a viable photocatalytic structure with potential practical uses.

Melamine was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Ethanol was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid and urea were purchased from Merck (Germany). Carmoisine (96 %) and indigo carmine dye purchased from QReC̈ (Asia) Sdn. Bhd., Malaysia. The remaining chemicals were all of the analytical grades. All of the commercially available compounds were of highly analytical purity and could be used right away without further purification. Milli-Q water purification system-filtered ultrapure water was used to produce and dilute each solution.

Alfa Aesar (located in Ward Hill, Massachusetts) supplied the melamine. Ethanol obtained from Sigma-Adri (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Both the citric acid and the urea werepurchasedfrom the German company Merck. These two dyes carmoisine and the indigo carmine dye were purchasedfrom the Malaysian company QReC (Asia) Sdn. Bhd. All of the compounds that were left were of analytical quality. Each solution was prepared and diluted with ultrapure water that had been filtered by a Milli-Q water purification system.

Powder patterns of X-ray diffraction were captured using Cu-K (=0.1546 nm) radiation and a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray diffraction instrument. The Joel/JEM 2100 electron microscope with thetransmission instrumentis used for the characterization of TEM. Field emission scanning microscopy (TESCAN-MIRA 3 LMH) was used to examine the surface morphology, and EDS QUANTAX was used to study the elemental composition. Using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 1000 with a scanning range of 350–4000 cm−1, FTIR data is collected from KBr discs. We use a Specord 210 and an Analytic Jena UV–Visible spectroscopy to capture UV-DRS and BET studies were conducted using BELSORP-max. TGA study (Model SDT Q 600) evaluated the thermal stability of GCN and CDCN nanocomposites (Fig. S1). Raman spectra were collected using Peak Seeker Pro TM with a laser power of 5–300 mW and a wavelength of 785 nm (Fig. S2). The Zeta potential analyzer Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern) was used to test the stability of GCN and CDCN nanocomposites.

Hydrothermal methods could be used for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this procedure, 25 mL of Double distilledwater was used to dissolve 5.0 g of citric acid together with different amountsof urea. Then, this entire solution was placed into Teflon-sealed in an autoclave of 100 ml capacity and heated to 190 °C for 5 h. Then the resultant solution was centrifugated at 15,000 rpm for about 15 min to removelarge particles present in the solution. Following centrifugation, the light brown solution was dialyzed for 12 h using a 1000 Da membrane, with Maliquewater andchanged every 2 h to ensure complete removal of small molecules. The product was further purified by filtering it via a 0.22 µm polyether sulfone (PES) membrane. This two-step approach successfully eliminated both bigger and smaller molecules from the reaction mixture. The CQD solution has been evaporated to dryness at 60 °C to generate CQD powder.

The process for the formation of graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) began withheating directly with melamine. Typically, 5.0 g of melamine was placed in a crucible made of alumina and heated for 4 h at a temperature of 550 °C. The synthesized GCN was then cooled to ambient temperature and crushed into powders.

One gram of GCNwas diluted into 90 mlof DDwater. The multilayered structure of GCNwas exfoliated and delaminatedusing ultrasonication. After that milli-que water was used to dissolve a known quantity of bulk NCQDs. The solution was ultrasonically treated for 15 min before being mixed with the GCNsolution. A homogeneous suspension was achieved afterstirring the resulting liquid for 30 min at room temperature. Then, for 4 h at 120 °C, the mixture had been hydrothermally heated in an autoclave. The obtained solution has been moved to a vacuum filter after being reduced to ambient temperature. After that collect the obtained material and wash it twice with purified water. Then the solution was kept in a 70 °C oven for overnight drying. Fig. 1 illustrates the hydrothermal approach to synthesizing CDCN composites.

Under UV light irradiation, the GCN, CQD, and CDCN materials served as catalysts to break down the CM & IC dyes. The current studywas conducted in a Heber reactor with continual stirring and UV lamps of 250 W and 365 nm were used. In this experiment, 1 mg/mlof catalysts were added to fifty milliliters of colored dye solution (10 ppm) in quartz tubes with a capacity of 50 ml volume. Then the solution was continuously agitated without interruption for 30 min in complete darkness without exposure to UV light to achieve a constant equilibrium. Then the solution was exposed to UV rays at 250 W UV tungsten lamb at regular intervals after reaching a stable level. Then samples were taken in a 2 ml- Eppendorf vial and the UV–vis Spectrophotometer was used to record the degraded de-colorization solution and record its absorbance, which was scanned at 200–800 nm.

The kineticrate constant of photocatalyticdegradation was calculated using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model, which is shown below
(1)
ln
C 0
/ C = k t

Additionally, the following equation was used to calculate the performance of photocatalytic degradation:
Degradation efficiency = C0 − C/C0 100 %,
The initial amount of the CM and IC dyes is Co, while its final concentration after exposure to light is C.

Studies of radical scavenging provided insight into the photodegradation mechanism of CM and ICdyesby identifying the radicles that are essential for the reaction. To prevent the loss of electrons, hydroxyl radicals, holes, and superoxide radicles during thephotodegradation of CM and IC dyes, a mixture of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), parabenzoquinone (PBQ), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) is used. Separately applied 0.2 mg/ml of CDCN NPs, and 2.0 mmol/L of radical scavengers were applied to the CM and IC dye solution before exposure the UV light. Using photodegradation efficiency calculations, we couldpinpoint the reactive species accountable for dye breakdown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recyclability and stability analysis of photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Toevaluate the synthesized photocatalysts' potential for reuse or recycling with GCN and CDCNcatalysts, the decolorization of CM and IC dyes was examined under conditionscomparable to exposure to ultraviolet radiation and constant dark adsorption for 30 min. The photocatalysts were removed from each cycle, rinsed with the deionized aqueous solution, centrifuged,and dried in an ovenbefore being employed againin the subsequent cycle. The reusability or recyclability of the photocatalysts was assessed and compared throughoutfive cycles of testing.

The XRD patterns were used as the first source of evidence that CDCN and GCN NPs had been synthesized effectively. The materials' crystalline nature and the structure of the synthesized nanomaterials were both revealed by the XRD analysis. Fig. 2
(a–b) displays the XRD patterns of pristine GCN and a range of CQD-modified GCN nanocomposites with varying loading percentages. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the pure GCN having(002) and (100) planes corresponding diffraction peaks at 27.2° and 13.2°. The multi-layer stacking arrangement of segments may be reflected in the major peak at 2Θ = 27.2°, which could be matched to (002) planes [41]. The(100) peakmay be related to the (2Θ = 13.2°) weaker plane due to the in-plane structural packing pattern of tri-s-triazine subunits [42] . The two distinct diffraction peaks at 2Θ = 27.2° and in Fig. 2(b) show that the CQD doping had no noticeable effect on the GCN crystal structure. Fig. 2(b) demonstrates that, in doping, CQD doesn’t have any appreciable effect on the crystalline size of GCN, as the major peaks at 2Θ = 27.2° and 13.2° of CDCNare specifically indexed to pure GCN (JCPDS Card No. 87-1526) [43] . The low amount of CQD doping level might clarify the lower crystallinity of CQDs seen in the XRD patterns of thedifferentratios oftheCDCNnanocomposite. In Fig. 2(b) the prominent diffraction peaks of GCNare consistent with those of CDCN (1:1), CDCN (2:1), and CDCN (1:2) peaks.

FT-IR method analyses the chemical composition of pure CQD, GCN, and CDCN Fig. 3 (a). Pure CQD revealed multiple peaks representing the oxygen functional group, particularly 1043 cm−1 (−C–O–C–), 1541 cm−1 C = O, and 1358 cm−1 (COOH), as seen in Fig. 3(a). These polar groups' hydrophilic properties increased the solubility and stability of the CQD, which may have improved its ability to transport electrons. The –C–NH–C vibration contributed to the 1181 cm−1 peak found in the non-oxygenated groups, whereas the two vibrations C–N and C = N generated around 1341 cm−1 and 1655 cm−1 [44,45] . The addition of CQD had no discernible effect on the rate of absorption peaks when compared to pure GCN. As a result, it was established that the hydrothermal doping of pure GCNwith the CQD did not affect the chemical framework of the CDCNcomposite photocatalyst. The presence of NH and OH function groups are responsible for the broad peaks ofabout 3600–3000 cm−1 [46] .

The spectral absorption properties of the obtainedCDCN catalystswere examined using UV–visible/DRSspectroscopy. The primary absorption edge of pristine GCNwas observed to be at around460 nm. The initial red absorption edge at 460 nm shifted slightly while increasingthe amount of CQDs incorporated into GCN ( Fig. 3(b)). This study suggests that whenever GCNis modified withCDCN, it can capture greater amounts of visible light. This is essential for boosting the photocatalytic performance of the materialssince improved visible light consumption can result in the production of enhanced photo generation of a pair of electron holes. GCN and CDCN had a maximum absorbance of around 370–380 nm and a predicted band gap value of around 2.72 eV, which is quite similar to reported studies. CDCN (1:1),CDCN (1:2), and CDCN (2:1)band gaps were 2.63 eV, 2.69 eV, and 2.55 eVrespectively. Fig. 3(c) graph suggests that there is a gradual narrowing of the band gap from 2.7 to 2.55 as CQD loading increases.

Fig. 4 illustrates how the distinctive shape and distribution of the GCN, CQD, and CDCN photocatalysts were investigated using various HR-TEMand FE-SEM images. Fig. 4 (a, b)'s TEM picture demonstrates the sphere-like morphologyform of CQD. The average diameter of the obtained CQD was around 3.5 nm shown in Fig. 4(c). Fig. 4 (a–e) displays TEM images of GCN, CQDs, and CDCN catalysts. Exfoliated layers of GCN NPs exhibit a wrinkly, two-dimensional lamellar structure, as shown in Fig. 4(d). The connection of CQDs to the surface and interlayer of GCN is shown in Fig. 4(e). The elemental composition of CDCN NCs was examined by EDAX analysis and was shown in Fig. 4(f).

The surface morphology of CDCNnanocomposites and GCNnanoparticleswas investigated with FESEM analysis. Fig. 5 (a–b) shows the FE-SEM image of GCN with a layered blocky structure with an uneven lamellar arrangement. On the other hand, the surface of CDCNnanocomposites was displayed in Fig. 5 (c–d). Some particle agglomeration can be observed, which probably occurred due tothe CQD doping process onto the GCN framework. This agglomeration could be attributable to interactions among CQDs and GCN during the hydrothermal process, which results in regional gathering of the nanocomposite particles.

To examine the elemental composition of the CDCNcomposite, EDX analysis was performed. According to the findings Fig. 5(h), the sample's composition containsCarbon, Nitrogen, and Oxygenhaving atomic and alsoweight percentages of 32.31 % (36.00 %), 61.85 % (59.11 %), and 5.84 (4.89 %), respectively. In addition to EDX, the elemental mapping was alsoperformedto verifythe existence of C, N, and O presence in the CDCN nanocomposites which are shown in Fig. 5 (e–g). In addition to theEDX, elemental mapping further confirmed the presence of C, N, and O presence in CDCN NCs. The C element was obtained from the amorphous carbon present in the GCN and also from citric acid throughout the CQD synthesis. Next, the N element was found in the GCN's tri-s-triazine structure and urea presence in CQD, whereas the small O concentration might be related to the presence of acidic species (–COOH) in the CQDs.

Since photocatalysis is a surface-dependent process, the surface area is important in determining photocatalytic activity. Whenever the outermost area is increased, the active sites become larger and more photo-catalyticallyeffective [47] . Fig. 6 (c) displays the N2 adsorption –as well as desorption isotherms for CDCN, which provide direct evidence of the material's BET-specific surface area. CDCN has a much higher BET-specificsurface area than GCN Fig. 6(d) suggesting that the CQDs are embedded in the GCN nanosheets of material.All the samples containing isotherms were generally of type IV, and each one had a loop of hysteresis at larger P/P0 distributions, which is indicative of their mesoporous nature. The total surface area of GCN and CDCN was 17.521 m2/g and 26.279 m2/g respectively. Additionally, the porosity of GCN and CDCNwas determined to be 3.816 nm and 1.422 nmrespectively, using the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore size. These findings imply that CDCN has a mesoporous character that could significantly improve photocatalytic efficiency when compared toGCN NPs.

Using a zeta sizer, the stability of the GCN and CDCN was assessed. In most instances, the zeta values of GCN & CDCN are calculated by using the surface charges. The Zeta values for pure GCN & CDCNwere −23.6 mV and −51.8 mV, accordingly Fig. 6 (a, b). It was discovered that GCNhad lower zeta potential values than doped material. So, the figure indicates that CDCN has more stability than pure GCN. The CM and ICdye is degraded more quickly when morenegatively charged ions arepresent on the surfaces of nanoparticles. The more negativeZeta valuedepicts the morenegatively charged ions on the surface of NPs, leading to faster degradation of CM and IC dye. CM & IC arecationic dyes, which improve the interaction between the catalystsand the dyes, therefore this is extremely significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of GCN, CDCN for the degradation of carmoisine
ce:section.ce:para: A typical UV–Vis spectrum of carmoisine (CM)solution under photoirradiation is depicted in Fig. 7 . Dye degradation is evidenced by a decline in the intensity of the major peak of absorption that corresponds to the dye during the reaction. Neither the visible nor the ultraviolet ranges show any new absorption bands. The rapid azo dye degradation by a reduction in the CM absorption peak at ƛ max = 517 nm. It has peaks of absorption at 517, 310, and 220 nm. The major peak at 517 nmis caused by N = N, C = N, and C = O. Chromophores going through n-π* transitions and π-π* transitions in aromatic rings cause absorbance peaks at 310 and 200 nm, which show that the dye is aromatic [48] .

CDCN nanocompositeswere used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of CM dye shown in Fig. 7(a) to produce typical UV–vis absorption spectra. Only 70 % of the CM dye's degradation occurs while it is exposed to the GCN catalyst, which causesprolonged degradation. However, when the CM dye was exposed to the CDCN in its unaltered form, practically 93.1 % & ofall of it was broken down. GCN takes 90 min to degrade completely, but in the case of the CDCN photocatalyst, the dye degrades completely in 60 min.

The photocatalytic performance of the food dyes always has a significant impact on the pH dye solution. Controlling the adsorption process and subsequent degradation method up on the dye’s solutions' initial pH. So here, using GCN, and CDCN catalysts, the influence of pH on the degradation of CM dye has been investigated. Changes in pH, influence the charge transfer by modulating the conduction and valence band's redox potential, which in turn influences the surface charge of the catalysts. With a specific amount of catalyst, GCN NPs and CDCN catalysts were used to study the impact of altering pH from 2 to 10, and a specific concentration of 10 ppm of CM dye was utilized to assess the variation in pH. Fig. 7(b) shows the diagram of the photocatalytic degradation of CM dye with increasing pH. When the dye solution's initial pH is raised, the photocatalytic degradation activity for GCN and CDCN rises for pH 2 and 3 before falling. While pH3 shows 71.3 % for GCN and 93.5 % degradation for CDCN catalyst. Then the degradation drops to 12 % and 7 % after that.

The influence of CM dye levels on the degradation of photocatalytic dyewas examinedby modulating the dye concentration levelfrom 10 ppm to 50 ppm. It has been demonstrated that the initial concentration level of dye considerably affects the percentageof CM dye degradation rate. Here, the percentage of degradation rate becomes dramatically lowered as the dye concentration increases as shown in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8 (a–c) shows the kinetic studyand percentage plot for thedegradation of CM dye at various concentrations (ppm) using GCN. For dye concentrations of 10, 20, 30,40, and 50 ppm, the percentage of degradation at 90 min is 69.5 %, 59.13 %, 47.8 %, 21.4 %, and 17.9 %, respectively. The kinetic study and percentages bar diagram for the CDCN degradation of CM dye at various concentrations are shown in Fig. 8 (b–d). The dye concentrations at 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 ppm are 93.5 %, 77.13 %, 55.85 %, 13.2 %, and 7 % respectively. Compared to GCN catalyst, CDCN has a high rate of degradation efficiency within a shorter period.

The overall kinetic rate of photocatalyticdye degradation was shown to dramatically slow down as CM dye concentration increased. This might be due to the excess dye molecules covering the catalyst’s surface, which could impede the breakdown of the CM dye molecules. Moreover, the dye solution turbidity may limit the overall light penetration, which would reduce the photon-dye molecular interactions on the surface of the catalyst. Consequently, it was discovered that 10 ppm of dye was the optimal concentration for the CM dye degradation process.

The photocatalystdosage affects both the rate of reaction and photocatalytic degradation. The amount of catalyst needed for the degradation of CM dye was investigated by testing a range of photocatalyst dosages from 10 to 50 mg/ML Within a constant concentration of 10 ppm and also dye at pH 3. A 250 W, 365-nm UV lamp was used to facilitate the degradation reaction. CMdye degradation rates as a function of catalyst dosage are graphically depicted in Fig. 9 (a). As displayed in Fig. 9(b), When the amount of GCN dosage rises from 10 mg to 50 mg/mL, the levels of photocatalytic degradation activity improved by 18 %, 35.08 %, 51.3 %, 63.71 %, and 74.46 %, respectively. As for the catalyst CDCN, the percentage of degradation increases from 15 %,38 % 62.7 %, 70 %, 78.7 %, and 93.5 % accordingly shown in Fig. 9 (c–d). This might be becauseofthe availabilityof extra reactive sites for the process of degradation, such as
O2 and
OH, which can be produced quickly. Theseresults are shown in Tables 1 and 2
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of different natures of the light source
ce:section.ce:para:
The intensity of the incident light mostly determines how many photons are absorbed by the photocatalytic substance. Tungsten filament lamps of varying power(125, 250) W/cm2 are used as the UV source for the photocatalytic dye degradation experiments depicted in Fig. 10
(a, b). After 60 min of exposure to low-power (150 W/cm2) UV lamb, an optimized dye concentration of 10 ppm, and 0.2 mg/ml of CDCN catalyst with pH3obtained only 70 % of degradation, while GCN catalyst obtained 59.1 % of degradation. However, for light intensities of250 W/cm2, a greater degradation of 93.55 % was detected during 60 s of exposure using a CDCN catalyst, and 70 % degradation was obtained by using a pristine GCN catalyst ( Fig. 10(b)). Tables 1 and 2 show the effects of diverse parameters on CM degradation in the presence of GCN and CDCN NCs.

This indicates that a UV-light sourcehas an immediate impact on the rate at which dyes degrade due to thegreater availabilityof photon quanta, which ultimately boosts the production of e−/h+ pairs by interfering with the active regions of the catalyst substance using a suitable doping agent or co-dopantequilibrium. In contrast to other light sources including visible light (33.12 %) and direct sunlight (7.85 %), UV light irradiation caused the maximum photocatalytic degradation of the CM dye (93.5 %), as displayed in Fig. 10(a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CDCN for the degradation of indigo carmine
ce:section.ce:para: Indigo Carmine is a poisonous pigment that mostly seems to be the effluents from industrial wastes that are dangerous to living things [49]. Fig. S3 (a–b) displays the optical spectrum of IC dye during the degradation process employing photocatalysts GCN and CDCN. First, adsorption–desorption equilibrium was reached by constantly stirring the dye solution withthe photocatalystin the dark for 30 min. Then, the intensity of the blue color IC solution diminished over time during the photocatalytic experiment. UV–vis spectroscopy of the reaction samples reveals a slight decrease in all the absorption bands (610, 286, 252, and 206 nm). GCN takes 90 min to degrade, while in the presence of CDCN, IC dye degrades completely in 60 min. When IC dye is exposed to aGCN catalyst, discoloration happens more slowly than with CDCN, and it is evident from the graph that only 75 % of degradation is taking place. However, about 96 % of the IC dye was completely broken down when it was exposed to the CDCN in its unaltered form.

All semiconductors are influenced by the pH level of thedye solution, particularly the surface charge of the semiconductor particles,size, and the energies of conductance and valence band. According to reports, the effectiveness of the photodegradation reaction is influenced by both acidic and basic media. It was discovered that the formation of both H + and OH − ions could accelerate the photodegradation of the aqueous dye solution. The photocatalytic degradation level of samples is shown in Fig. 11
(a, b) as an illustration of pH (ranging from 2.0 to 10.0), under UV irradiation. The percentage degradation first slowly increased and then decreased with arise in pH, reaching its highest value (97 %) at pH 7. However, when the pH level is above 7, the surface charge is positive, which increases Columbic repulsion among the components of the dye and the surface of the catalyst causes a noticeable drop in efficiency [50,51] .

From a pH range of 2–10, 10 ppm of IC dye solution was alsotested for its absorbance change rate and degradation percentage using GCN and CDCN photocatalyst (optimum quantity). Fig. 11(a) depicts the IC absorption rate vs time for a range of pH levels. When the pH is 7, dye solution degradation is more effective. It is discovered that quicker and the maximum degradation of dye occurs at pH 7.0, with GCN 80 % and CDCN96 % dye degradation attained within 90 and 60 min oftheirradiation period shown in Fig. 11(b). However, the degradation pattern shows that as solution pH increases (from 2.0 to 6.0), degradation efficiency increases from 70 to 88 % after, with a maximum at pH 7.0, later it again decreased.

The impact of the initial concentration level on the degradation of IC dye was investigated by varying the dye concentration from 10 to 50 ppm with a particular amount of catalyst loading at a fixed pH of 7and using ultraviolet (UV) illumination for 0–90 min. Fig. 12
(a–c) shows the kinetic plot and bar diagram for thedegradation efficiency of IC dye at various concentrations (ppm) using GCN and CDCN catalysts. Fig. 12 (b–d) demonstrates the various concentrations of GCN catalyst from 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 ppm, the percentages of photocatalytic degradation activity studied within 90 min respectively, 75 %, 55 %, 33.1 %, 129.2 %, and 11.2 %. The percentage efficiency of CDCN catalytic degradation is 96 %, 82.3 %, 61.3 %, 22 %, and 14 % in dye concentrations of 10–50 ppm within 60 min. As the concentration of dye in a solution increases, less light can penetrate it. This meant that less number ofphotonscould stimulate the GCN and CQD band gaps. Degradation efficiency, therefore,decreases with increasing dye concentration. At constant catalyst loading, an increase in dye content reduces the quantity of light available to excite catalyst particles.

Dye degradation is shown to be dose-dependent on the photocatalyst. Dye degradation rates rise with increasing amounts of catalyst in heterogeneous photocatalysis [52]. Indigo carmine dye's reaction rate and degradation efficiencyare significantly affected by the catalyst loading. The photodegradation of the IC dye was measured over a 90 and 60-minute period to investigate the influence of the GCN and CDCN nanocomposite dose on the IC dye degradation. In a degradation reaction with a constant amount of ICdye (10 ppm) at pH 7 and different amounts of catalyst (0.2–1 mg/ml),were utilized. The percentage of degradation performance of the CDCN increases from 29 % to 96 % while the GCN catalyst degradation efficiency increases from 12 % to 81 %. Fig. 13
(a–d) displays the kinetics plot of IC dye degradation utilizing GCN and CDCN catalyst loadings (from 0.2 to 1 mg/ml). The reported literature survey on the degradation of IC dye using GCN with different types of catalysts are shown in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of different natures of the light source
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic efficiency of GCN and CDCN nanocomposites for the degradation of IC dye has been investigated under two different UV light, sunlight, and visible light irradiation conditions. Under all four light sources, GCN and CDCN demonstrated improved photocatalytic activity. By using the CDCN catalyst, exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light resulted in a maximum of 96 % degradation using 250 W, and for 150 W UV light was used to degrade 82 % of IC dye at a greater degradation rate than both visible light (45.5 %) and sunlight (9.8 %) (Fig. 14 (a)). Compared to the CDCN catalyst GCN catalyst's rate of degradation is low. As shown in Fig. 14(b) UV light at 150 W& 250 W obtained 71.5 % and 81.2 % of degradation. When it is exposed to visible light and sunlight only 17.1 %, and 8.1 percentage of degradation occurred within 90 min and the detailed results are shown in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the photocatalytic activity of dyes and comparison table
ce:section.ce:para: When GCN catalyst is doped with CQD its catalytic property is doubled in both CM & IC dye. From the experiment analysis, it's clear that the degradation efficiency of the CDCN catalyst 2 times more than the pristine GCN catalyst in both dyes.

CDCN NC photocatalytic activity against carmoisine and indigo carmine dye was investigated. The results demonstrate the nanocomposite's excellent catalytic properties towards both kinds of organic dyes, demonstrating its adaptability for use in the catalytic degradation of a wide variety of organic dyes. In Table 5 , we can see how CDCN's photocatalytic efficiency towards IC and CM dye compares to that of other catalysts that have been analyzed already. The data unmistakably show the photocatalyst's superior ability to remove the color from IC and CM dye in water-based solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of reactive species and photocatalytic degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Schemes 1 and 2
suggested the mechanismfor CDCN photocatalyst-mediated food dyephotodegradation. In the presence of UV light and having a wavelength of 335 nm, CDCN photocatalyst generates holes in the valence band (VB) and electrons on the conduction band (CB) of GCN.The band gap data suggest that GCNwill cause e− and H+ whereas CQD doesn't because CQD has a higher band gap (3.3 eV) that necessitates more energy from the incident light than GCN does (2.72 eV). High photocatalytic efficiency is also achieved due to the flow of electrons from the Conduction Band of GCNinto the Conduction Band of CQD, which inhibits the recombination process.

As shown in Scheme 2, there are multiple pathways through which the excitation can break down the food dyes into carbon dioxide and water. Both the direct reduction of these dyes and the reduction of oxygen to superoxide radicle species have the potential to act as reactive radical species. Another method is using holes; therefore, they either directly oxidize the dye, or oxidize water to produce hydroxyl radicals, which then degrade the CM/IC dyes as reactive radical species [64].

To investigate the photocatalytic mechanism, we used radical trapping studies on CDCN NCsto identify the key active components involved in the degradation process [65]. Reactive hydroxyl species were generated during thedecomposition of CMand IC dye. It has been identified via different scavengers (H+,
OH,
O2, and e − ), including ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Isopropyl alcohol(IPA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), and silvernitrate (AgNO3). Additionally, Fig. 15(a) illustrates the efficiency of photocatalytic degradationwith and without scavengers. When scavengers were applied, they were shown to reduce the degradation performance of CM and ICdye from 89 % to 49 % to 35 % to 20 %, in the following order:EDTA > PBQ > AgNO3 > IPA. The fact that the degradation efficiency is substantially decreased to just 20 % when IPAscavengers are added illustrates the significance of
OH in photocatalysis. When AgNO3 and PBQ were added as electron and superoxide radicle scavengers, IC and CM dye photodegradation dropped to 35 % and 49 %. further demonstrating the importance of electrons and superoxide radiclesin the degradation process. Furthermore, EDTA usedas ahole scavenger reduces IC and CM dye degradation to 84 %, suggesting that H+ doesn’t have any role in this method.

Upon exposure to light, Electrons (e−) and holes (h+) are produced. Because of the high mobility of the electron carriers and the disparities in their energy levels, the recombination of these photoinduced charge carriers can be minimized [66]. As shown in Scheme. 1 when exposed to light, CQD and GCN were induced to generate e+/h+ in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB). Electrons from photoexcited GCN went to CQD, while holes moved in the opposite direction, from CQD to GCN. Then the electrons reacted with the oxygen molecules in the dye solution to form superoxide radical (
O 2 ), while the holes reacted with H2O to form hydroxyl radical (
OH). The CM/IC food dyemolecule was quickly oxidized by the strong and intenseoxidant radical of
OH. In the presence of UV rays, we suggested the following plausible mechanism for the degradation of CM/IC food dyeswith CDCN photocatalyst.

Hazardous intermediate compounds can ariseas a result of the photodegradation process. Most probably, TOC measurements were conducted to assess the mineralization degree of CM & IC dyes using CDCN nanocomposite under UV ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. The TOC removal efficiency was estimated in these trials using the equation (Eq.10).
(10)

%
= T O
C 0
- T O
C t
/ T O
C 0
× 100
where TOC0 and TOCt, respectively, represent the total organic carbon concentrations before and following a particular point in the degradation process. whereas TOC0 and TOCt, respectively, represent the total organic carbon concentrations before and following a particular point in the degradation process. The results obtained, which are displayed in Fig. 17 (a) show that the percentage removal efficiency of TOC increases with degradation time and reaches its maximum after 60 min of photodegradation. In the case of IC dye, 97 % of TOC removal occurred in 60 min, while 70 % of TOC removal occurred in CM dye within 60 min. The percentage removal efficiency of TOC by CDCN NCs using Ultra-pure, tap water, and seawater in CM dye, and IC dyes are shown in Fig. 17 (b, c). This demonstrates the fact that in the investigated photodegradation process, IC &CM dyes and their intermediates eventually get converted to carbon dioxide and water. Therefore, it can be said that the CDCN nanocomposite photodegrades IC & CM dyes under UV light radiation, resulting in a substantial amount of mineralization. The method proposed here can be utilized to effectively remove CM & IC dyes and their associated byproducts from water solutions.

To test the particle’s stability, the procedure has been applied more than four times with the recovered CDCN nanocomposites. After the dye degradation procedure, these catalysts were centrifugated and repeatedly washed with DDW. The next day, it was dried at 60 °C for the entire night. The recovered photocatalyst was used once more in the degradation reactions. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the CDCN nanocomposites is approximately four cycles (Fig. 17a). The CDCNsample offers the maximum photodegradation of CMat (70 %) and IC at(96 %). CM and IC had a second-cycleelimination were 68.5 %and 94.3 %, respectively degradations for the third cycle and four cycles were 60.3 %, and 89.3 %, and for CM dye and IC dye were 57.1 %, and 87.9 %, respectively. Fig. 18 (a) depicts a degradation versus the number of cycles that were plotted.

Furthermore, any phase shift that occurred in the nanoparticles was analyzed by powder XRD to verify the long-term stability of CDCNnanocompositesafter fourcycles Fig. 18(b). The FT-IR and XRD of newly prepared and recovered CDCN after numerous degrading process cycles were compared to evaluate the stability of CDCN Fig. 18 (b–c). Further supporting the material's structural stability,TEM images taken before and after use, revealed no appreciable morphological modifications within CDCN nanocomposites after thefour cycles shown in Fig. 18 (d–e). The results demonstrate that there is no appreciable variation between newly produced and recovered catalysts. As a result, the catalysts CDCN are both extremely stable and can be used as photocatalysts for the degradation of CM and IC dyes.

An LC-MS analysis was conducted to identify the photocatalytic degradation products of the Carmoisine dye (CM). The LC-MS spectra of the untreated CM dye (Fig. S4(a)) and its degradation products after light radiation (Fig. S4(b)) were compared. These analyses were carried out using a gradient system of H2O, EtOH, ACN, and IPA, with an unsprayed ion source. Based on the LC-MS data, the potential degradation products of CM dye, inferred from their mass values, are presented in Fig S4. The proposed mechanisms for the breakdown pathways of CM dye are illustrated in Fig. S5. The degradation occurs under light exposure, leading to the formation of smaller molecules due to the action of hydroxyl radicals (
OH).

The degradation of Carmoisine dye begins with the cleavage of the azo bond (–N = N–), the most reactive group in the dye molecule, resulting in naphthalene derivatives. The oxidative degradation pathway of carmoisine, potentially leading to complete mineralization, likely follows three stages. First, oxidative cleavage produces all the components present in two sections of a and b (Fig. S5). Next, these intermediates undergo hydroxylation, forming polyhydroxylated derivatives that result in structures A (5,8,-hydroxy-2-nitronaphthalene-1-olate), C (5-hydoxy-4-nito-2-sulfonato benzoate), and D (naphthalene-1-sulfonate). The polyhydroxylated and quinoid derivatives are unstable and undergo oxidative ring-opening reactions, resulting in the formation of short-chain carboxylic acids. Additionally, desulfonation combined with the hydroxylation of 2,3,4-trihydroxy-naphthalene-1-sulfonic acid anion produces compound (B) and oxalic acid (E).

In Fig. S6(a–b) the fragmentation and breakdown products of IC dyes are illustrated. LC-MS analysis identified the degradation products and proposed mechanisms involved in the photocatalytic degradation process, as detailed in Fig. S7. The pathway includes all detected intermediates and highlights the role of active radicals, particularly hydroxyl radicals generated during the process, as key oxidants. Other weaker oxidants, such as H2O2 and HO2, may also contribute.

According to LC-MS analysis, isatin 5-sulfonic acid (m/z 226) was identified as the primary aromatic product, formed when a hydrogen radical attacked the C = C bond in indigo carmine (Fig. S7). The isatin 5-sulfonic acid then lost SO4 2− and was converted to isatin. Further oxidation of the intermediates produced a mixture of carboxylic acids and amines. These compounds were ultimately broken down into inorganic molecules, including carbon dioxide, ammonium, nitrate, and others.

In conclusion, GCNhas been effectively functionalized with CQDs to highly dispersed CDCN composites suitable for CM and IC degradation inUV light. To identify the optimum conditions for the efficient degradation of food dyes, the presence of different physio-chemical factors on the photo-degradation properties of the GCN and CDCN dopedphoto-catalysts was investigated. Characterization investigations using techniques including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirmed that pure GCN and CDCN compositeshad been successfully modified. The CDCN optical properties, as measured by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance analysis, improved as CQD mass loading was applied, and the photocatalyst showed increased absorption intensity. Using a Tauc plot, we found that itresulted in the lowest band gap values for doped CDCN particleswhen compared to pure GCN. CDCN'sphotocatalytic efficiency was demonstrated by its ability to degrade CM and ICdyes.

To study the effects of photodegradation, in terms of photocatalytic efficiency, the CDCN composite performed better than pure GCN for both CM and IC dyes. After 90 & 60 min of UV irradiation, the CDCN composite effectively removed 93.5 % of CM and 96 % of IC dyes. The rate constants for the reactions involving IC and CM dye with CDCN catalyst were calculated to be 0.00113 and 0.00124 respectively. Comparedto published reports, it’s the first time CDCN catalyst was used for the photocatalytic degradation of Carmoisine (CM) and indigo carmine dye (IC). Compared with the otherprecursors GCN, the CDCNcomposite was shown to have enhanced degrading capability when used as a photocatalyst on IC and CMdyes. Experiments using radical scavengers demonstrated the importance of radicles in the photocatalytic decomposition mechanism.

Here, Hydroxyl radicals play a crucial place in the plausible mechanism of photodegradation of IC and CM dye utilizing CDCN photocatalyst. It can be regarded as a flexible photocatalyst for the degradation of indigo carmine and carmoisine dye in industrial effluents due to its inexpensive cost, simple synthesis technique, maximum rate of photocatalytic effectiveness, and excellent reusability. New opportunities for customizing hetero-structured catalysts with efficient catalytic performances and further uses in environmental remediation are anticipated as a result of this work.
